title: Japanese Chicken Teriyaki
description: Boneless chicken thighs glazed in a sweet-salty soy sauce until caramelised and lacquered — Japan's most-loved weeknight protein. The two-step cook (sear then glaze) keeps the meat juicy while delivering a sticky, restaurant-quality finish without any special equipment.

ingredients.name: sugar (or mirin if available)
ingredients.notes: mirin gives a more authentic gloss; if using, reduce soy to 3 tbsp

ingredients.name: rice vinegar
ingredients.notes: balances the sweetness

ingredients.name: toasted sesame seeds
ingredients.notes: for garnish

ingredients.name: spring onion, thinly sliced
ingredients.notes: for garnish

steps.text: Stir together soy sauce, sugar, honey, rice vinegar and ginger-garlic paste in a bowl to make the teriyaki glaze. Taste — it should be salty, sweet and slightly tangy. Set aside two tablespoons of the glaze separately for basting later.

Pat the chicken dry, score the thicker pieces lightly with a knife, and season both sides with a small pinch of salt. Pour the remaining glaze over the chicken and toss to coat. Leave to marinate for 10 minutes at room temperature.

Heat oil in a heavy-bottomed pan or tawa over medium-high flame. Lift the chicken out of the marinade, letting excess drip off, and lay it flat in the pan. Cook without moving for 5–6 minutes until the underside is deep golden brown and releases cleanly.

Flip the chicken and cook the second side for 4–5 minutes. Pierce the thickest part with a skewer — the juices should run clear and the internal texture should feel firm with a little give. If the pan gets dry before the chicken is cooked through, splash in 2 tablespoons of water.

Pour the reserved glaze over the chicken and let it bubble for 1–2 minutes, tilting and spooning the sauce over the pieces as it reduces to a sticky, lacquered coating. Watch carefully at this stage — the sugar can burn in seconds.
Rest the chicken for 3 minutes off the heat before slicing diagonally into thick pieces. Arrange over steamed rice and spoon any remaining pan sauce on top. Finish with sesame seeds and spring onion.

substitutions.substitute: firm tofu (pressed, cut into thick slabs) for a vegan version — reduce cook time by 2 minutes per side
2 tbsp coconut aminos + 1 tsp salt for a soy-free glaze
maple syrup or date syrup
substitutions.notes: same quantity, slightly different flavour profile

substitutions.substitute: fresh lemon juice
substitutions.notes: use the same amount; adds brightness

culturalNote: Teriyaki is a fundamental Japanese technique rather than a single dish — the name combines teri (gloss from the sauce) and yaki (grilled or broiled). Home cooks in Japan apply the same glaze to fish, pork and vegetables throughout the week.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A thick tawa or cast-iron kadai works perfectly in place of a Western skillet. Soy sauce (shoyu) is available at most large Indian grocery stores and online. Mirin can be replaced by dissolving 2 tsp sugar in 1 tbsp water with a splash of vinegar (sirka). Serve over steamed sona masoori or ponni rice for a satisfying meal.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Japanese Onigiri with Tuna
description: Hand-pressed Japanese rice balls filled with seasoned canned tuna and wrapped in crisp nori — the definitive Japanese packed lunch. Warm rice is the secret to good shape; once formed, onigiri travel well and taste just as good hours later.

ingredients.name: short-grain rice or sushi rice
ingredients.notes: sona masoori is a good Indian substitute; avoid basmati as it won't bind

ingredients.name: canned tuna or salmon, drained well
toasted sesame oil
ingredients.notes: mixes into filling for flavour

ingredients.name: nori sheets, cut into strips
ingredients.notes: each strip wraps the base of one onigiri

ingredients.name: toasted sesame seeds
ingredients.notes: mixed into filling or pressed onto outside

ingredients.name: water
ingredients.notes: for cooking rice

steps.text: Rinse the rice in cold water, changing it three or four times until the water runs mostly clear. Drain, then cook with 500 ml water in a saucepan: bring to a boil, stir once, cover tightly, reduce flame to the lowest setting and cook for 12 minutes. Remove from heat and let it steam, still covered, for 10 more minutes. Do not lift the lid.
While the rice cooks, break up the drained tuna with a fork, mix in the sesame oil, sesame seeds and a small pinch of salt. The filling should be lightly seasoned — the rice will also be salted.

Once the rice is cooked and just warm enough to handle, wet both palms with cold water and rub a generous pinch of salt across them. Scoop about three-quarters of a cup of rice onto one palm and press a small hollow in the centre.

Spoon 1–2 tablespoons of tuna filling into the hollow. Bring the rice up around the filling from all sides, then cup both palms around the mound and press firmly with moderate force — rotate the ball while squeezing to compact it evenly into a triangle or rounded shape. The onigiri should feel solid, not crumbly.

Wrap a strip of nori around the base and lower sides of each onigiri, pressing gently so it sticks to the warm rice. Serve within 4 hours at room temperature, or pack immediately into a lunchbox; refrigerating makes the rice hard.

substitutions.substitute: mashed avocado with a drop of soy sauce for a vegan filling
substitutions.notes: changes the diet type to vegan

substitutions.substitute: thin strips of roasted papad or toasted nori-flavoured rice crackers for a makeshift wrap
a few drops of any neutral oil plus a tiny drop of toasted sesame seeds
sona masoori cooked slightly wetter than usual — it clumps well once warm

culturalNote: Onigiri have been eaten in Japan for over a thousand years and remain the country's default grab-and-go food, sold in every convenience store. The triangle shape represents the three-dimensional form of mountains, which were considered sacred in early Japanese culture.
regionalVariation: Fillings vary widely by region and season — umeboshi (pickled plum) is the classic; salmon, kombu, and mentaiko (spiced roe) are also standard. The nori can be kept separate and wrapped just before eating for maximum crunch.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Cook rice in a pressure cooker with 2 cups water for 2 whistles — it comes out slightly sticky, which is ideal for shaping. Sona masoori rice binds well when warm; rinse it thoroughly. Nori sheets are available at metro supermarkets and online grocery stores in India. Canned tuna (Tata's Real Good Fish or similar) is sold at most D-Mart or Reliance Fresh outlets.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Japanese Miso Soup
description: A quietly nourishing bowl of fermented soybean broth with silken tofu and wakame — the anchor of every Japanese home meal. The key is to never boil the soup after the miso is added, preserving the living cultures and keeping the flavour rounded rather than harsh.

ingredients.name: white or red miso paste
ingredients.notes: white miso is sweeter and milder; red miso is saltier and more intense

ingredients.name: firm tofu, cut into 1 cm cubes
dried kombu (kelp), for dashi
ingredients.notes: a 10 cm strip is enough; use kombu labelled 'dashi kombu'

ingredients.name: dried wakame seaweed
ingredients.notes: rehydrates to about 4x its volume

ingredients.name: shiitake mushrooms, whole or sliced
ingredients.notes: adds depth to the dashi; dried shiitake gives more umami

steps.text: Combine water, the kombu piece and shiitake mushrooms (if using) in a saucepan. Place over medium-low heat and bring slowly to a bare simmer — 10 minutes. Remove the kombu just before the water reaches a full boil; leaving it in too long makes the dashi bitter.

Remove the mushrooms (slice them and return to the pot if you like). Add the dried wakame directly to the hot dashi — it rehydrates in under 2 minutes. Add the tofu cubes and let the soup return to a gentle simmer for 2 minutes.

Turn off the heat. Place the miso paste in a small ladle or fine strainer and submerge it in the soup, pressing and stirring until fully dissolved with no lumps. Never boil the soup once miso is in — heat destroys its flavour and probiotics.

Ladle into bowls immediately and scatter spring onion over the top. Serve as a starter or alongside rice and any main dish. Miso soup does not reheat well; make only as much as you will drink at one sitting.

substitutions.substitute: cubed paneer (drained well) for a non-vegan version with a similar texture
1 tbsp soy sauce plus a pinch of salt for a lighter emergency broth — flavour will be simpler
a strip of dried kombu from any Korean or Japanese grocery; if unavailable, simmer 1 tsp soy sauce in water for a basic umami broth
1 tsp instant vegetable stock powder to deepen the dashi if kombu is not available

culturalNote: Miso soup has been a daily staple in Japan for over five hundred years; it appears at breakfast, lunch and dinner without ever feeling repetitive because the add-ins rotate with the seasons — daikon and tofu in winter, young onions in spring, corn in summer.
regionalVariation: Kyoto-style miso soup uses white (shiro) miso for a delicate, slightly sweet broth; Nagoya's signature Hatchomiso soup uses an aged, intensely savoury red miso. The choice of dashi also varies — bonito-based kombu-katsuobushi dashi is most common in eastern Japan, while the Kansai region favours lighter kombu alone.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Miso paste is available at health food stores and on Amazon India (brands like Hikari or Marukome). If unavailable, substitute 1 tbsp soy sauce plus a pinch of urad dal paste dissolved in hot water for a rough umami base. Tofu is sold as 'soya paneer' or 'bean curd' in metro markets. The whole soup cooks in one saucepan with no special equipment.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Japanese Chicken Katsu Curry
description: Crispy breaded chicken cutlets served over steamed rice and blanketed in a golden, mildly spiced Japanese curry sauce built from a scratch butter-flour roux. The sauce is sweeter and less hot than Indian curry, and the combination of crunchy katsu with silky gravy is deeply satisfying.

ingredients.name: boneless chicken breasts or thighs (2 large pieces)
maida (plain flour), for coating and roux
ingredients.notes: about 50 g for coating, 30 g for the roux

ingredients.name: panko breadcrumbs or fine dried breadcrumbs
ingredients.notes: panko gives the lightest crust; regular breadcrumbs also work

ingredients.name: cooking oil (for frying)
ingredients.notes: for shallow or deep frying; use less for shallow frying

ingredients.name: potato, peeled and cut into 3 cm chunks
curry powder (mild Japanese-style or any mild blend)
ingredients.notes: S&B brand if available; or use a mild homemade blend of cumin, coriander and turmeric

ingredients.name: soy sauce
ingredients.notes: adds umami depth to the curry

steps.text: Pound each chicken piece to an even 1 cm thickness using a rolling pin or meat mallet — this ensures even cooking when frying. Season both sides with salt.

Set up three shallow bowls: one with flour, one with beaten egg, one with breadcrumbs. Coat each chicken piece first in flour (shake off excess), then dip completely in egg, then press firmly into breadcrumbs on both sides. Let the coated pieces rest on a plate for 5 minutes so the coating adheres.

Meanwhile start the curry: heat 1 tbsp butter in a heavy-bottomed pot over medium flame. Add onions and cook for 10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until soft and golden. Add potato and carrot and stir for 2 minutes to coat in the butter.

Push the vegetables to one side of the pot. Add the remaining 2 tbsp butter to the empty space, let it melt, then stir in the flour. Cook the butter-flour paste for 1 minute until it turns a faint biscuit colour, then add the curry powder and fry for 30 seconds until fragrant.

Pour in the stock and soy sauce gradually, stirring constantly to incorporate the roux without lumps. Bring to a simmer, cover and cook for 15 minutes until the potato and carrot are completely tender. The curry should coat a spoon thickly. Adjust salt.

Shallow-fry the katsu: pour about 2 cm of oil into a kadai or frying pan over medium-high heat. When a breadcrumb sizzles on contact, add the chicken and fry for 4–5 minutes per side until deep golden and cooked through. Drain on kitchen paper.

Slice the katsu into 2 cm strips. Plate a mound of steamed rice, lean katsu slices against it, and spoon the curry generously over everything. Serve immediately — the coating loses its crunch if it sits in the curry for long.

substitutions.substitute: thick slices of paneer or firm tofu, coated and fried the same way, for a vegetarian katsu curry
finely crushed cornflakes for a lighter, gluten-free-ish crust
2 tbsp oil for the roux — the curry will be slightly less rich but otherwise identical
equal parts cumin powder, coriander powder, turmeric, with a pinch of cardamom and clove for a DIY Japanese-style spice blend
substitutions.notes: milder and less heat-forward than standard Indian curry powder

culturalNote: Japanese curry arrived in the country via the British Raj in the late 19th century and has since evolved into something entirely its own — milder, sweeter, and thicker than South Asian curries. Katsu curry is a fixture on school lunch menus and a comfort staple for millions of Japanese families.
regionalVariation: Some households add a small piece of dark chocolate or a spoon of ketchup to the curry for extra depth and sweetness. Tonkatsu (pork cutlet) is the more traditional protein, though chicken katsu has become equally popular.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Fry the katsu in a kadai with 2–3 cm of oil over medium-high heat — it works exactly like making cutlets. Japanese S&B curry powder is available at specialty stores; a mild DIY blend of 1 tsp each cumin, coriander, turmeric plus a pinch of cardamom works well. The roux step is essentially white sauce (béchamel) technique — standard in any Indian kitchen that makes pasta bake or bread rolls.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Japanese Yaki Udon
description: Thick wheat noodles stir-fried with vegetables and a salty-savoury soy-sesame sauce — a quick Japanese street food that comes together in under 15 minutes once the prep is done. High heat and a confident hand with the spatula are the only secrets.

ingredients.name: udon noodles (fresh, frozen or dried)
ingredients.notes: dried udon must be boiled first; fresh or frozen go straight into the wok

ingredients.name: toasted sesame oil
ingredients.notes: added at the end for fragrance

ingredients.name: sesame seeds
ingredients.notes: for garnish

steps.text: If using dried udon, bring a large pot of water to a boil and cook the noodles until just tender, about 8–10 minutes. Drain and rinse under cold water to stop cooking. Fresh or frozen udon just needs a quick soak in boiling water for 1–2 minutes to loosen.
Heat cooking oil in a large kadai or wok over high flame until you see wisps of smoke. Add the sliced onion and stir-fry for 1–2 minutes, keeping it moving, until the edges begin to colour.

Add carrots and mushrooms and toss for 2–3 minutes over high heat until the mushrooms are wilted and fragrant. The key is to keep the flame high throughout — lower heat makes vegetables steam and turn soggy instead of getting a slight char.
Add the shredded cabbage and toss everything together for 1–2 minutes until the cabbage softens at the edges but still has a little crunch.

Add the udon noodles and pour soy sauce over the entire mixture. Use tongs or two spatulas to toss everything vigorously so the noodles separate and coat evenly. Stir-fry for 2 more minutes on high heat.
Remove from heat, drizzle sesame oil over the top and grind over plenty of black pepper. Toss once more, then pile into bowls and scatter spring onion and sesame seeds on top. Eat immediately.

substitutions.substitute: thick Chinese hakka noodles (boiled) or even thick cooked wheat vermicelli (semiya)
substitutions.notes: closest texture widely available in India

substitutions.substitute: 2 tbsp coconut aminos + 1 tsp salt for a soy-free version
omit or use a tiny drop of mustard oil for a pungent Indian twist — it changes the flavour significantly
substitutions.notes: toasted sesame oil is the authentic choice

substitutions.substitute: thinly sliced capsicum or broccoli florets for variety

culturalNote: Yaki udon emerged as a practical, no-waste dish in the years immediately after World War II when wheat noodles were more accessible than rice in many Japanese cities. It remains one of the most popular teppanyaki (iron griddle) dishes at Japanese festivals and casual restaurants.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A large kadai on maximum flame is ideal for wok-style stir-frying — pre-heat it for 2 minutes before adding oil so it is genuinely hot. Thick Chinese hakka noodles from the local store are the best substitute for udon. Soy sauce is widely available in India; sesame oil (gingelly oil / nallennai) is stocked in most South Indian grocery stores.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Japanese Tamagoyaki (Rolled Omelette)
description: A gently sweet Japanese rolled omelette cooked in thin successive layers and folded into a compact log — served as a lunchbox staple, sushi topping or breakfast side. The patient layering technique, not the ingredients, is what creates the distinctive striped cross-section.

ingredients.name: sugar (or mirin if available)
ingredients.notes: mirin gives a more rounded sweetness; plain sugar is a workable substitute

ingredients.name: water
ingredients.notes: makes the egg softer and more delicate

ingredients.name: spring onion, thinly sliced
ingredients.notes: garnish

steps.text: Crack eggs into a bowl and add sugar, soy sauce and water. Stir gently with chopsticks or a fork — the goal is to combine the yolks and whites without incorporating air or making foam. The mixture should be uniform in colour but not frothy.

Place a non-stick pan or tawa over medium-low heat and add a thin film of oil, spreading it with folded kitchen paper. Pour in roughly one-third of the egg mixture — just enough to coat the base in a thin, even layer.

Let the egg set at the edges while the centre is still slightly wet, about 30 to 45 seconds. Using a spatula, roll the egg from the far end of the pan toward you in two or three folds, forming a loose log at the near edge. Do not wait for the egg to be fully firm before rolling — soft is correct.

Slide the log to the far end of the pan. Add a small drop of oil to the exposed surface, then pour in the second third of egg mixture, gently lifting the existing roll so the new liquid egg flows underneath it. Cook until the surface is barely set, then roll the existing log back over the new layer.

Repeat with the remaining egg mixture, rolling the growing log one more time to incorporate it. The finished roll should be plump, with visible white and golden layers when cut. Press gently in a bamboo mat or wrap in cling film and let it rest for 1 minute to compact the shape.
Slice the roll crosswise into pieces about 2 cm thick. The interior should show distinct layers. Arrange on a plate and garnish with spring onion. Serve warm or at room temperature.

substitutions.substitute: 1 tbsp mirin if available, for a rounder, less sharp sweetness
a pinch of salt for a plainer, cream-coloured tamago without dark streaks
no direct substitute — this dish is egg; paneer bhurji is a thematically different dish
a very light coating of butter for a richer flavour

culturalNote: Tamagoyaki appears in the Japanese bento box tradition going back centuries and is considered a benchmark of cooking skill — chefs at sushi restaurants are sometimes tested on their tamagoyaki before any other dish. The sweet version is standard in the Tokyo and Kansai home kitchen.
regionalVariation: Osaka-style tamagoyaki is thicker and sweeter; Tokyo-style (dashimaki tamago) uses dashi stock instead of water, giving a more savoury, aromatic result.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A non-stick tawa or small frying pan works perfectly — no special tamagoyaki pan is needed. Cook on medium-low flame so each layer sets gently without browning too quickly. The rolled technique is similar to making a thin roti-style omelette and rolling it — Indian cooks will find the motion intuitive. Soy sauce is available at most modern supermarkets and online.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Japanese Oyakodon (Chicken and Egg Rice Bowl)
description: Thinly sliced chicken and barely-set eggs simmered together in a sweet-savoury dashi broth and served over warm steamed rice. The name means 'parent and child' in Japanese, referring to the hen and egg cooking together — and the half-set, custardy egg texture is the heart of the dish.

ingredients.name: boneless chicken thighs, sliced thinly against the grain
dashi stock or light chicken/vegetable stock
ingredients.notes: instant dashi powder dissolved in water works well

ingredients.name: sugar (or mirin)
ingredients.notes: mirin gives more depth; plain sugar is a workable substitute

ingredients.name: spring onion, sliced on the diagonal

steps.text: Cook rice in a saucepan with 2.5 cups water: bring to a boil, cover, reduce to lowest flame for 12 minutes, then rest covered for 10 minutes. Keep covered and warm — this is the base of the bowl.
Combine dashi stock, soy sauce and sugar in a wide, shallow pan. Stir until the sugar dissolves, then bring to a simmer over medium heat.
Add sliced onion to the simmering broth and cook for 3 to 4 minutes until softened and slightly translucent. The onion absorbs the broth and adds body to the topping.

Scatter the chicken slices evenly over the onion in a single layer — do not stir. Simmer for 5 to 7 minutes until the chicken is fully cooked through with no pink remaining, turning once if the pieces are thick.
Beat the eggs just enough to break the yolks with a few streaks of white still visible. Pour the egg in a slow circular motion over the chicken, starting from the outer edge inward. Do not stir.

Cover the pan and cook for 1 to 2 minutes: the whites should be set at the edges but the centre should still wobble gently — this half-custardy texture is the signature of oyakodon. The residual heat finishes the cooking once it sits on the rice.

Divide rice between two large bowls and carefully slide the chicken-egg topping over each, distributing it evenly. Scatter spring onion on top and serve immediately — the topping softens the rice quickly and tastes best eaten fresh.

substitutions.substitute: thinly sliced firm tofu pan-fried briefly for a vegetarian version — the dish becomes tamagodon
1 tsp instant dashi powder dissolved in 200 ml water, or plain water with an extra tsp of soy sauce for a simpler broth
1 tbsp mirin for a more rounded sweetness with less sharpness
any medium-grain rice or sona masoori cooked slightly wet so it absorbs the broth readily

culturalNote: Oyakodon is one of Japan's most beloved don (rice bowl) dishes and is considered a mother-cooking archetype — simple, warming and comforting. It is a fixture in Japanese family restaurants and school cafeterias, and remains one of the most searched home recipes in Japan.
regionalVariation: The Osaka version uses a sweeter, lighter broth; Tokyo oyakodon is more savoury. Some families add a splash of dry white wine or rice wine to the broth for fragrance.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Cook the topping in a flat-bottomed kadai or wide saucepan so the egg sets in an even layer. Dashi is ideal but light chicken stock — or a Maggi stock cube dissolved in water — gives a very acceptable result. The half-cooked egg texture is similar to an egg curry where the yolk is just barely set — Indian cooks will immediately recognise the target consistency.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Japanese Vegetable Tempura
description: Mixed vegetables coated in a barely-mixed, ice-cold batter and fried until delicately crisp and pale gold — tempura is defined by the lightest possible coating, one that shatters rather than crunches and lets the vegetable flavour speak. The discipline is restraint: cold batter, hot oil, quick fry.

ingredients.name: mushrooms (shiitake or button), whole or halved
cornflour (cornstarch)
ingredients.notes: key to a light, shattering crust

ingredients.name: ice-cold water
ingredients.notes: place a bowl of water over ice; cold temperature is non-negotiable for tempura batter

ingredients.name: oil (for deep frying)
ingredients.notes: neutral oil such as sunflower or refined groundnut

ingredients.name: dashi or light vegetable stock (for dipping sauce)

steps.text: Pat all vegetables completely dry with kitchen paper — surface moisture creates steam and prevents the batter from crisping. Cut everything to roughly uniform thickness so pieces cook in similar time. Lay out on a tray ready to coat.
Make the dipping sauce first: combine soy sauce, stock and sugar in a small saucepan, stir over low heat for 1 minute until sugar dissolves. Remove from heat and cool to room temperature.

Make the batter immediately before frying: crack the cold egg into a bowl, add the ice water and stir briefly. Tip in flour and cornflour all at once and mix with a fork using a folding motion — no more than 10 strokes. Visible lumps are correct; overmixing develops gluten and makes the batter heavy.

Heat oil in a kadai or deep saucepan to 170 to 175 degrees C. Test with a drop of batter — it should sink slightly, rise immediately and sizzle actively. Fry in small batches to maintain temperature; crowding cools the oil.

Dip each vegetable piece into the batter, letting excess drip off, and lower it gently into the oil. Fry for 2 to 3 minutes per batch, turning once, until the coating is pale gold and the sound changes from loud sizzling to a quieter crackle. Drain on a wire rack.

Between batches, skim the oil surface clean of loose batter crumbs which burn and bitter the oil. Serve tempura within 5 minutes of frying alongside the dipping sauce — tempura loses its crunch quickly and does not reheat well.

substitutions.substitute: replace with an extra 30 ml of ice water for a vegan batter that is slightly lighter and less golden
rice flour (chawal ka atta) for a gluten-free batter that is even lighter and more delicate
substitutions.notes: available at any Indian grocery store

substitutions.substitute: potato starch in the same quantity for a very similar effect
any firm vegetable — sweet potato, baby corn, sliced onion rings or pumpkin all fry beautifully in tempura batter

culturalNote: Tempura was introduced to Japan by Portuguese missionaries in the 16th century and evolved into one of Japan's most refined cooking traditions. Traditional tempura restaurants in Tokyo serve seasonal ingredients to a single counter of diners, treating each piece as a course.
regionalVariation: Tokyo's Edo-style tempura uses a thinner batter fried in sesame oil, producing a slightly darker, aromatic result. In Osaka, shrimp is the iconic tempura ingredient; vegetables dominate the home-cooking tradition.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A deep kadai is the perfect vessel for tempura frying. Keep a kitchen thermometer handy; 170 degrees C is the target. Cornflour (cornstarch) is widely available at Indian grocery stores. The cold-batter discipline is the main technique to internalise — keep your mixing bowl over ice if possible. Serve with soy sauce as a dip if proper tsuyu (dashi-based) is not available.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Japanese Cucumber Maki Rolls
description: Seasoned sushi rice and crisp cucumber matchsticks rolled inside nori sheets — the cleanest, most beginner-friendly sushi roll. The slightly tangy, sticky rice is the craft element; the rolling technique takes one practice round before it feels reliable.

ingredients.name: short-grain rice or sona masoori rice
ingredients.notes: long-grain or basmati will not bind properly; use the stickiest variety available

ingredients.name: cucumber, seeds scooped out, cut into thin matchsticks
rice vinegar
ingredients.notes: regular white vinegar can substitute at slightly reduced quantity

ingredients.name: toasted sesame seeds
ingredients.notes: pressed onto the outside of the roll

ingredients.name: toasted sesame oil
ingredients.notes: a small drizzle over the cucumber before rolling lifts the filling

steps.text: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, drain, and cook with 500 ml water: bring to a boil, stir once, cover tightly, reduce to the lowest flame and cook for 12 minutes. Turn off the heat and leave covered for 10 more minutes without lifting the lid.

Mix rice vinegar, sugar and salt together, stirring until dissolved. Spread the hot cooked rice on a wide tray and pour the dressing over it. Fold through with a spatula in fanning motions — do not stir vigorously. Cool the rice to body temperature before rolling; hot rice tears the nori.

Place a sheet of nori shiny-side down on a bamboo mat or clean cutting board. Wet your hands lightly to prevent sticking. Spread about three-quarters of a cup of seasoned rice in an even layer over the nori, leaving a 2 cm bare strip at the far edge.

Arrange four or five cucumber matchsticks in a straight line across the near edge of the rice. If using sesame oil, drizzle a few drops over the cucumber now. Sesame seeds can be scattered across the rice at this stage.

Lift the near edge of the mat and roll it firmly over the cucumber in one confident motion, pressing gently to compact as you go. When the near edge of nori meets the rice on the far side, pause and squeeze the roll through the mat to compact it, then continue rolling to seal the bare nori strip at the far end.

Set the roll seam-side-down for 1 to 2 minutes to let the nori seal. Dip a sharp knife in cold water before each cut and slice each roll into 6 to 8 pieces with a single forward pull rather than a sawing motion. Serve with soy sauce for dipping.

substitutions.substitute: if nori is unavailable, arrange the seasoned rice on a lettuce leaf and roll as a fresh hand-roll — the flavour is lighter but it works
ripe mango strips for a sweet tropical roll, blanched carrot batons, or avocado if available
2 tbsp white vinegar (sirka) plus an extra pinch of sugar — sharper than rice vinegar but workable
sona masoori cooked slightly wetter than usual — it clumps when seasoned warm; avoid basmati entirely

culturalNote: Kappa maki (cucumber roll) is named after the kappa, a water-sprite in Japanese folklore said to be fond of cucumber. It remains one of the three most popular sushi rolls in Japan, prized for its clean, refreshing flavour between richer pieces.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Sona masoori rice cooked wet and well-rinsed is the best Indian substitute for sushi rice — it binds once seasoned and cooled. Nori sheets are stocked at premium supermarkets in Indian metros and are widely available online. White vinegar (sirka) can replace rice vinegar at slightly reduced quantity. A clean kitchen towel stretched flat can substitute for a bamboo rolling mat.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Japanese Gyoza (Pan-Fried Dumplings)
description: Thin-skinned dumplings filled with seasoned pork and cabbage, fried flat-side-down until deeply golden then steamed in the same pan to cook the tops — the dual-texture result (crispy base, tender top) is entirely distinctive. Momo wrappers from Indian Tibetan shops make an excellent ready-made shortcut.

ingredients.name: maida (plain flour), for wrappers
ingredients.notes: or use 25 to 30 ready-made momo or gyoza wrappers — this skips wrapper prep entirely

ingredients.name: hot water (for dough)
ingredients.notes: hot water makes the dough smooth; skip if using ready-made wrappers

ingredients.name: minced pork or chicken keema
ingredients.notes: chicken mince (keema) is a lighter substitute

ingredients.name: cabbage, very finely shredded
ingredients.notes: salt and squeeze out all moisture before mixing — wet cabbage makes soggy gyoza

ingredients.name: water (for pan-steaming)
ingredients.notes: added to pan to steam the tops of the gyoza after initial fry

ingredients.name: sesame seeds
ingredients.notes: for dipping sauce garnish

steps.text: If making wrappers from scratch: mix maida with hot water and a pinch of salt into a firm dough. Knead for 5 minutes until smooth, cover with a damp cloth and rest for 15 minutes. If using ready-made momo wrappers, skip to step 2.

Salt the shredded cabbage, let it sit for 5 minutes, then squeeze out all the liquid with your hands — do this firmly until no more water comes out. Combine squeezed cabbage with pork, ginger-garlic paste, soy sauce, sesame oil and spring onion. Mix until the filling holds together when pressed.
If using homemade dough: pinch off walnut-sized balls and roll each into a thin circle about 8 cm across. Keep the edges slightly thinner than the centre so the pleated crimps are not too thick.

Place one heaped teaspoon of filling in the centre of each wrapper. Dampen the edges with a little water, fold the wrapper in half to enclose the filling and press to seal the top. Pleat the front edge with four or five overlapping folds, pressing each one firmly — this is the characteristic gyoza crimp.

Heat oil in a non-stick pan or flat tawa over medium-high heat. Arrange gyoza flat-side-down in a single tight layer without touching. Fry for 3 to 4 minutes until the bases are deeply golden and release cleanly from the pan.

Pour 60 ml of water carefully into the hot pan and cover immediately with a lid — it will spit and steam vigorously. Steam for 4 to 5 minutes over medium heat until the water has fully evaporated and you can hear the frying sound return.
Remove the lid and cook for 1 more minute to re-crisp the bases. Serve immediately, golden-side-up, with a dipping sauce of equal parts soy sauce and rice vinegar scattered with sesame seeds.

substitutions.substitute: chicken keema for a lighter filling, or firm tofu finely crumbled with mushroom for a vegan version
substitutions.notes: press tofu completely dry before using

substitutions.substitute: ready-made momo wrappers (available at Tibetan restaurants and select Indian supermarkets) — this shortcut cuts prep by 20 minutes
a few drops of mustard oil for a bold Indian accent, or omit for a simpler filling
finely chopped spinach or bok choy — squeeze moisture out just as thoroughly before mixing in

culturalNote: Gyoza arrived in Japan through Chinese jiaozi after World War II and became thoroughly domesticated — thinner-skinned, more garlicky, with the pan-steam cooking method as its signature. Gyoza is the standard accompaniment to a bowl of ramen in Japanese casual dining.
regionalVariation: Utsunomiya city in Tochigi Prefecture is so associated with gyoza that it has a gyoza statue and hosts an annual gyoza festival. The Utsunomiya style fries the dumplings on both sides; standard gyoza is only crisped on the bottom.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Momo wrappers, available at Tibetan-run eateries and some supermarkets in North India, are the ideal shortcut — they are slightly thicker than gyoza skins but fry and steam beautifully. A flat non-stick tawa works well for frying. The fry-steam-crisp sequence mirrors the covered kadai technique used for certain Indian fried breads — the logic will feel immediately familiar.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Japanese Shoyu Butter Corn Rice
description: Steamed rice tossed with corn kernels cooked in butter until lightly caramelised, then finished with a drizzle of soy sauce — a beloved Japanese home comfort that uses a handful of pantry ingredients to produce something far more satisfying than the sum of its parts. The butter-soy combination is an iconic Japanese flavour pairing.

ingredients.name: short-grain rice or sona masoori rice
sweet corn kernels (fresh, frozen or canned)
ingredients.notes: fresh corn cut off the cob has the best texture; canned works well drained

steps.text: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook with 600 ml water in a covered saucepan over medium-low heat: bring to a boil, stir once, cover tightly, reduce to lowest flame and cook for 12 minutes. Remove from heat and rest, covered, for 10 minutes.

While the rice cooks, melt butter in a wide pan or tawa over medium heat. Add corn kernels in a single layer and cook without stirring for 2 to 3 minutes until the undersides are lightly golden and you can smell a nutty, slightly sweet aroma.

Stir the corn and cook for 1 more minute. Pour the soy sauce directly over the corn and stir quickly — it will spit and caramelise against the hot pan within 30 seconds. Remove the pan from heat immediately.

Fluff the cooked rice with a fork or rice paddle. Add the corn and all the buttery, savoury pan juices to the rice and fold through gently with a scooping motion so the corn is evenly distributed without crushing the grains.

Taste for seasoning — the soy sauce should be sufficient but you can add another splash if needed. Plate and scatter spring onion generously over the top. Finish with a grind of black pepper and serve immediately.

substitutions.substitute: coconut oil for a dairy-free version with a subtly sweet note, or ghee for a richer Indian-inflected flavour
substitutions.notes: ghee changes the flavour profile but pairs beautifully with soy

substitutions.substitute: frozen peas or edamame (shelled soya beans) for variety; both work well with the butter-soy base
coconut aminos plus a pinch of salt for a soy-free version
substitutions.notes: similar savoury depth with a slightly sweeter note

substitutions.substitute: sona masoori or any medium-grain rice; avoid basmati as it doesn't absorb the corn juices the same way

culturalNote: Butter and soy sauce is one of Japan's most beloved flavour pairings — the combination appears on corn at summer festivals, in ramen shops as an add-on, and in many home rice preparations. Japanese corn is famously sweet, making this dish almost dessert-like in contrast to the savoury soy.
regionalVariation: Hokkaido, Japan's northernmost island, is famous for sweet corn and butter dishes — visitors often try 'corn ramen' with butter and corn as the default topping. The rice version is a home-cooking adaption of those flavours.
indianKitchenAdaptation: This recipe requires no special equipment — a saucepan for rice and a tawa for the corn is all you need. Amul butter works perfectly. Sweet corn (bhutta dana) is available frozen or canned at any Indian supermarket; you can also cut fresh corn off the cob and use it immediately. Soy sauce is available nationwide.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Japanese Curry Rice from Scratch
description: A thick, silky Japanese vegetable curry built from a butter-flour-curry-powder roux — sweeter, milder and rounder than Indian curry, with a sauce that coats the rice in a glossy, almost velvety layer. Making the roux from scratch takes no more than 5 extra minutes but produces a depth of flavour that boxed roux cannot match.

ingredients.name: potato, peeled and cut into 3 cm chunks
butter
ingredients.notes: split as 2 tbsp for the vegetables and 2 tbsp for the roux

ingredients.name: maida (plain flour)
ingredients.notes: forms the roux that thickens the curry

ingredients.name: curry powder (mild)
ingredients.notes: use a mild blend; Japanese S&B brand if available, or any mild curry powder

ingredients.name: soy sauce
ingredients.notes: adds umami depth characteristic of Japanese curry

ingredients.name: honey
ingredients.notes: authentic Japanese touch; gives the curry a round, gentle sweetness

steps.text: Melt 2 tbsp butter in a heavy-bottomed pot over medium heat. Add sliced onions and cook for 12 to 15 minutes, stirring every few minutes, until deep golden and beginning to caramelise. This long cook unlocks the sweetness that defines Japanese curry.
Add potato and carrot pieces and stir for 2 to 3 minutes, coating them in the buttery onion base. Pour in about 100 ml of the stock and scrape up any caramelised bits stuck to the pot bottom.

In a separate small saucepan, melt the remaining 2 tbsp butter over medium-low heat. Add flour and stir constantly for 2 minutes until it turns a pale biscuit colour and smells slightly nutty. Add curry powder and cook for 30 more seconds until fragrant — this is your scratch roux.
Pour the remaining stock gradually into the roux, whisking or stirring vigorously after each addition to prevent lumps. Once smooth, pour this curry sauce into the vegetable pot. Stir to combine.

Bring to a gentle simmer, add soy sauce and honey, cover loosely and cook for 20 to 25 minutes until the potato and carrot are completely tender when pierced with a knife. Stir every 5 minutes and adjust heat so the curry barely bubbles — it burns if left unattended.

Taste and adjust salt. The curry should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon; if too thin, simmer uncovered for 5 more minutes. Serve in a generous pool next to a mound of steamed rice on the same plate — Japanese curry is always served beside the rice, not on top.

substitutions.substitute: 3 tbsp oil for a dairy-free version — the roux will be slightly less silky but very acceptable
equal parts cumin powder, coriander powder and turmeric with a pinch of cardamom — gives a warming spice profile that reads as Japanese-ish
substitutions.notes: avoid hot chilli powder; Japanese curry is intentionally mild

substitutions.substitute: 1 tsp sugar or a small piece of grated apple (if available) for the characteristic sweetness
water plus a stock cube or 1 tbsp soy sauce for a simpler but still flavourful base

culturalNote: Japanese curry (kare) arrived via Britain in the late 1800s, absorbed Indian spice ideas through the imperial trade route, and became entirely its own thing over the following century. Today it is considered a national comfort food — Japan consumes more curry per capita than India on a per-meal basis when school and office lunches are counted.
regionalVariation: Some families add a square of dark chocolate, a spoon of ketchup or Worcestershire sauce to deepen the sauce — these are all traditional home cook's tricks. Keema (minced meat) curry, cheese curry and seafood curry are popular variations using the same roux base.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A heavy-bottomed kadai works well for the caramelised onion base. The roux step is identical to making bechamel (white sauce) — any Indian cook who has made pasta bake knows this technique. Japanese mild curry powder (S&B brand) is available at specialty stores in India; or build a DIY mild blend with equal parts jeera powder, dhania powder, haldi and a pinch of elaichi. Amul butter works perfectly.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Japanese Zaru Soba (Cold Buckwheat Noodles)
description: Chilled buckwheat noodles drained on a slatted tray and served with a cold soy-based dipping sauce — Japan's signature summer dish and one of the most elegant examples of cooking by subtraction. The cool noodles, nutty from buckwheat, are lifted by the umami of the dip and finished with spring onion and sesame.

ingredients.name: soba noodles (buckwheat noodles)
ingredients.notes: use 100% buckwheat for gluten-free; most commercial soba contains some wheat

ingredients.name: kombu (dried kelp), for dipping sauce
ingredients.notes: a 10 cm strip; makes the cold dashi base for the dip

ingredients.name: sugar (or mirin)
ingredients.notes: balances the salt of the soy

ingredients.name: toasted sesame oil
ingredients.notes: a few drops tossed through the drained noodles prevent sticking and add fragrance

ingredients.name: water
ingredients.notes: for the dipping sauce dashi and for boiling noodles

steps.text: Make the dipping sauce: combine 300 ml water and the kombu strip in a small saucepan. Bring slowly to a simmer over low heat — 10 minutes. Remove the kombu, add soy sauce and sugar, stir and simmer for 1 more minute. Pour into a bowl and refrigerate until completely cold, at least 20 minutes.

Bring a large pot of water to a boil (no salt — soba noodles need unsalted water). Add the soba noodles and cook for 3 to 5 minutes per the package timing, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking. Taste one at 3 minutes — it should be tender with just the faintest resistance.

Drain the noodles immediately and plunge them into a large bowl of ice water. Rub the noodles gently with your hands under the cold water — this removes the surface starch and is essential for the characteristic clean, non-sticky texture.
Drain the chilled noodles very well in a colander, shaking out as much water as possible. If using sesame oil, drizzle it over and toss lightly to coat each strand.

Arrange the cold noodles in a mound on a plate or in a bowl. Pour the chilled dipping sauce into small cups or a shallow bowl on the side. Serve with spring onion and sesame seeds in separate small dishes — diners add them to the dip according to taste.
To eat: pick up a small bundle of noodles, dip the ends briefly into the sauce (not the whole strand), and eat in one or two slurps. The noodles should stay cold throughout the meal — work quickly.

substitutions.substitute: 100% buckwheat soba for a gluten-free version; check the label as many brands blend with wheat flour
substitutions.notes: in India, look for 'sobha noodles' at Japanese or Korean import stores

substitutions.substitute: 1 tsp instant dashi powder dissolved in 300 ml water if kombu is unavailable — gives a very similar base for the dipping sauce
tamari (wheat-free soy sauce) for a gluten-free dipping sauce, keeping the same quantities
crushed roasted peanuts for a different but pleasant flavour note alongside the dipping sauce

culturalNote: Zaru soba — named for the bamboo 'zaru' strainer on which the noodles are traditionally served — is one of Japan's quintessential summer foods, sold at every soba restaurant and convenience store from June through September. Slurping soba loudly is considered appreciative rather than rude in Japan.
regionalVariation: Nagano Prefecture in the Japanese Alps is considered the home of soba culture, producing some of the finest buckwheat in the country. In Tokyo, classic soba shops prepare fresh-milled noodles daily; the Kansai region uses soba less often and prefers udon.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Soba noodles are available at Korean and Japanese import stores in Indian metros and online. The recipe requires only a saucepan and no special technique beyond chilling. Instant dashi powder (sold alongside soba noodles at import stores) makes the dipping sauce fast. If kombu is unavailable, a light soy-based sauce with a pinch of seaweed flakes also works. Serve on a hot summer day as an alternative to any Indian cold meal.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Japanese Chahan (Fried Rice)
description: Fluffy, separated fried rice with scrambled egg, spring onion and a clean soy-sesame finish — chahan is simpler and less oily than Chinese fried rice, with a neutral base that makes it the ideal companion to any Japanese main dish. Day-old refrigerated rice is the non-negotiable secret to grains that separate rather than clump.

ingredients.name: cooked rice (preferably day-old, refrigerated)
ingredients.notes: day-old rice is drier and fries without steaming — fresh rice produces mushy chahan

ingredients.name: spring onion, finely sliced (white and green parts separated)
mushrooms (shiitake or button), finely diced
ingredients.notes: adds umami; omit for a simpler, purer chahan

ingredients.name: toasted sesame oil
ingredients.notes: added at the end to preserve fragrance

ingredients.name: sesame seeds
ingredients.notes: garnish

steps.text: If the rice has been refrigerated, break up any large clumps with your fingers before cooking. Beat the eggs well with a pinch of salt and set aside. Make sure all other ingredients are prepped and within arm's reach — chahan moves fast once the pan is hot.

Heat a large kadai or wok over maximum flame for 2 minutes until very hot. Add cooking oil and swirl to coat. Pour in the beaten eggs and stir rapidly for 30 to 40 seconds, breaking them into large, soft, barely-set curds — remove from the pan while they are still slightly wet.

Add a little more oil if the pan looks dry. Add the white parts of the spring onion and the mushrooms (if using), and stir-fry for 1 to 2 minutes over high heat until the mushrooms begin to shrink and colour.

Add all the cold rice to the pan. Use the back of a spatula to press and break up any remaining clumps, tossing the rice continuously over high heat for 2 to 3 minutes until each grain is separate, hot and slightly toasted on the outside.

Pour soy sauce around the edge of the pan (not directly on the rice) so it hits the hot metal and caramelises slightly. Toss everything to distribute the sauce evenly, then return the scrambled egg to the pan and fold through the rice.

Turn off the heat. Drizzle sesame oil over the rice and toss once more. Taste and adjust with salt and black pepper. Scatter the green parts of the spring onion and sesame seeds over the top. Serve immediately — chahan loses its texture as it sits.

substitutions.substitute: crumbled firm tofu stir-fried briefly for a vegan version — season tofu with a pinch of turmeric for colour
coconut aminos for a soy-free version in the same quantity
a few drops of mustard oil for a pungent Indian note; or omit — the rice is already well-seasoned
frozen peas, finely diced carrot, or corn kernels — add at the same stage and cook until heated through

culturalNote: Chahan was adapted from Chinese chao fan (fried rice) in the early 20th century and became a Japanese staple in its own right — lighter in oil and seasoning than its Chinese cousin, and typically made at home using the leftover rice from the night before. Many Japanese ramen restaurants serve half-portions of chahan as a side.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A large pre-heated kadai on highest gas flame is the ideal Indian vessel for chahan — the technique is virtually identical to making egg rice or leftover rice stir-fry, which is already common in Indian homes. Day-old sona masoori or ponni rice works excellently. Soy sauce and sesame oil are the only special-pantry items; both are available at major Indian supermarkets. Add a pinch of white pepper for a restaurant-style touch.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen
